title: The Problems of Nutritious-Meal and Anganwadi Workers: What Is This Government's Approach?
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: Nutritious-meal and Anganwadi workers across Tamil Nadu have announced a rail-blockade protest today. The State has about 68,000 nutritious-meal centres and 35,000 Anganwadi centres, employing roughly 250,000 people. About 40,000 posts are vacant and have remained unfilled for three years. As a result, one nutritious-meal organiser must supervise more than five centres. The workers have therefore been demanding that vacancies be filled immediately.

They have also been protesting for regulated pay scales, an end to handing some centres to private operators, employment for the heirs of workers who die in service, and a statutory family pension, among other demands.

Under the DMK government, the allowance for vegetables and firewood for nutritious-meal beneficiaries was increased from 35 paise to 44 paise on 18 July 2007. The feeding allowance was then raised from 44 to 70 paise for primary-school children, to 80 paise for middle-school children, to 80 paise for Classes IX and X, and to 56 paise for children aged two to five in child-care centres.

For primary pupils, cooking oil was increased from one gram to three grams per day. For middle-school pupils, rice rose from 100 to 150 grams, oil from one to three grams and vegetables from 50 to 60 grams. By an order dated 24 September 2010, pupils in Classes IX and X also received 150 grams of rice instead of 120, three grams of oil instead of one, and 60 grams of vegetables instead of 50.

Among the heirs of school nutritious-meal workers who died in service, 57 were appointed as organisers, eight as cooks and eleven as cooking assistants, a total of 76 compassionate appointments under the DMK government.

From 25 October 2007, provision was made for compassionate appointment of heirs of workers who died in service. Under it, 29 people were appointed as Anganwadi workers and thirteen as Anganwadi assistants.
The DMK government in office from 27 January 1989 to 30 January 1991 classified these workers as permanent part-time employees of their respective panchayats.
It ordered three months of paid maternity leave for women nutritious-meal workers.

During the DMK government of 13 May 1996 to 13 May 2001, the Family Welfare Fund was extended to nutritious-meal workers. The heirs of those who died in service received Rs 1.5 lakh, as government employees did.
The retirement age of cooking assistants was raised from 55 to 58. A Special Provident Welfare Fund was begun for nutritious-meal and Anganwadi workers, with a government contribution of Rs 5,000.

The previous AIADMK government did not raise their pay scale even once. The DMK government raised it three times. Their scale of Rs 200-5-250-10-400 was first raised in 1998 to Rs 600-10-700-20-1100, retrospectively from 1 January 1996. It was raised again from 1 September 2006 to Rs 1,300-20-1,500-25-2,000. Under the Pay Commission recommendation it was raised a third time to Rs 2,500-5,000 with grade pay of Rs 500, again with retrospective effect from 1 September 2006.

Under the DMK, workers completing ten years received one increment and those completing twenty years another. Cooks, cooking assistants and Anganwadi assistants who had worked on consolidated pay under the AIADMK were placed on special pay scales.

Under the AIADMK, they received no allowance other than dearness allowance. From 1 June 2009, the DMK government gave them monthly house-rent and city compensatory allowances, a Rs 100 medical allowance and a Rs 2,000 festival advance.

They received no monthly pension under the AIADMK. The DMK government introduced pensions of Rs 700, Rs 600 and Rs 500 respectively for retired nutritious-meal organisers and Anganwadi workers, cooks and first-grade assistants, and cooking assistants and second-grade assistants.
The AIADMK gave them no promotions. Under the DMK, nutritious-meal assistants were promoted as cooks and cooks and assistants as organisers. In all, 1,020 cooks and assistants were promoted.

New DMK benefits included a General Provident Fund from 1 April 2010, a Special Provident Welfare Fund of Rs 10,000, a Family Welfare Fund of Rs 1.5 lakh, and coverage up to Rs 2 lakh under the Tamil Nadu Government's new health-insurance scheme.

Nutritious-meal and child-centre workers who completed the Diploma in Teacher Education were appointed as secondary-grade teachers. After the DMK government ordered teaching appointments for Anganwadi workers with B.Ed. degrees, lists of 59 qualified Anganwadi workers and 405 nutritious-meal workers were sent to the Teachers Recruitment Board on 4 October 2010 for a special examination and appointment.

During five years of AIADMK rule from 2001 to 2006, new appointments were made in only 7,655 posts. From 2006, the DMK filled a total of 56,833 posts in nutritious-meal and child-care centres, including 6,408 Anganwadi workers, 1,991 mini-Anganwadi workers and 13,167 assistants. Organisers and Anganwadi workers who had earned between Rs 1,488 and Rs 2,158 a month under the AIADMK received between Rs 4,911 and Rs 5,471 under the DMK.

Their present Tamil Nadu pay is higher than in any other State, and no other State offers the same range of benefits and schemes. A dedicated Tamil Nadu Nutritious Meal and Integrated Child Development Scheme Employees' General Provident Fund was also implemented.

In gratitude for the DMK government's measures, the workers invited me to a great appreciation meeting at Valluvar Kottam on 21 November 2009. At that meeting alone, I announced fifteen new measures for them.

Despite all these benefits, when they announced a protest for 30 August 2010, Jayalalithaa issued a statement the next day condemning "the Chief Minister of the minority DMK government, Karunanidhi," for allegedly unleashing repression instead of discussing the workers' just demands with union leaders. She demanded that dismissals and disciplinary action against State and district office-bearers be withdrawn immediately and promised: "MGR's rule will soon blossom, and the grievances of nutritious-meal workers will be resolved."
Her government did come. Were their grievances resolved? Under the DMK, that protest was settled through talks.
Even the AIADMK election manifesto promised priority decisions on nutritious-meal workers' employment and pay. Was that promise honoured?

M. Varadarajan, joint convener of the Federation of Tamil Nadu Nutritious Meal and Anganwadi Associations, has said that while the workers protested under the previous government, Jayalalithaa called their demands just, urged talks with their leaders and promised to fulfil them in her election pledge. Yet after three years, she had done nothing, had not called the unions for talks, and the Director of Social Welfare was instead continually threatening workers not to join the protest. What is the government's answer?

Jayalalithaa, who advised us while the DMK governed, is now reportedly using officials to deny permission for the protest and circulate instructions for disciplinary action against employees who leave headquarters without prior notice. Neither the minister's grant reply on 15 July 2014 nor the Chief Minister's Rule 110 announcements on 24 July contained anything for these workers.
At least now that they have announced a protest, the government should invite their unions for talks, reach an amicable settlement, and announce decisions on their demands.
With affection,
M. K.
12 August 2014
